title: DriftBench Code Drift Detection
subtitle: 54 code patches with ground-truth drift labels for LLM evaluation
description:
DriftBench is a benchmark for evaluating whether AI models can detect code drift — when a code patch subtly violates the original intent, project conventions, or introduces quality issues while appearing functional.

The dataset contains 54 scenarios (27 golden + 27 drift) across real-world TypeScript and Python repositories. Each scenario includes:

- **intent**: The original coding task description
- **patch**: A unified diff (git patch)
- **has_drift**: Ground truth boolean label
- **drift_type**: Category of drift (security, logic, stale, architecture, pattern, standard)
- **category**: Drift category for classification tasks

Drift categories covered:
- **security_drift**: Disabled protections, exposed secrets, unsafe operations
- **logic_drift**: Incorrect control flow, wrong variable scope, missing error handling
- **stale_drift**: Using deprecated/legacy patterns (var instead of const, CommonJS instead of ESM)
- **architecture_drift**: Circular imports, wrong abstraction layer, coupling violations
- **pattern_drift**: Inconsistent with project conventions
- **standard_drift**: Naming violations, incorrect exports

Part of the Rigour project: https://github.com/AashishNandakumar/rigour

resources.description: DriftBench evaluation scenarios. Each row is a JSON object with task_id, scenario_id, name, repository, category, intent, patch (unified diff), has_drift (boolean), drift_type, and expected_gate fields.
